final_output:
# Vector Database Performance Comparison for Production RAG Systems
## Definitive Technical Report for $500K Infrastructure Investment Decision

---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

This report compares five vector databases—**Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus, and pgvector**—for production RAG workloads across three scale tiers (1M, 100M, 1B vectors). It synthesizes evidence from ANN-Benchmarks (independent), Qdrant's vendor benchmarks, Zilliz's VectorDBBench, Supabase's pgvector benchmarks, and Pinecone's published documentation.

**The most critical finding: no single independent benchmark covers all five systems at equivalent hardware, production settings, and scale.** At 100M and 1B vectors, **zero independent benchmark data exists for any system.** Every recommendation at those tiers is necessarily grounded in architectural analysis and vendor claims rather than empirical measurement.

### Recommendation Summary

| Scale | Primary Recommendation | Confidence | Basis |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Small** (<1M, <$1K/mo) | **pgvector** (Postgres-native teams) or **Qdrant** (dedicated vector DB) | **Medium** — supported by multiple single-node benchmarks at 1M-10M | Empirical data (B.1) |
| **Medium** (100M, $1-10K/mo) | **Qdrant** or **Milvus** (self-hosted); **Pinecone Serverless** (managed) | **Low-Medium** — extrapolated from ≤10M benchmarks + architectural analysis | Architecture (B.2) + limited data |
| **Large** (1B, $10K+/mo) | **Milvus/Zilliz Cloud** or **Pinecone** | **Low** — no independent performance data at this scale | Architecture only (B.3) |

**Fiduciary warning**: A $500K commitment at medium or large scale should be preceded by a **funded proof-of-concept benchmark** on your actual workload. This report identifies which candidates merit that investment, but cannot substitute for it.

---

## PART A: ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK

### A.1 Comparison Dimensions

| Dimension | Metric | RAG Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Query Latency** | P50, P95, P99 (ms) | RAG retrieval is on the critical path before LLM generation (~500-2000ms). Vector search adding >100ms P95 is material. |
| **Throughput** | Queries per second (QPS) at target recall | Determines concurrent user capacity and cost-per-query |
| **Recall** | Recall@10 at fixed QPS target | Incorrect retrieval → hallucinated or irrelevant LLM output. The defining quality metric for RAG. |
| **Filtered Search** | QPS and recall with metadata filters (1%, 10% selectivity) | 50-80% of production RAG queries filter by tenant, document source, date, or permission scope |
| **Ingestion Throughput** | Vectors/sec during concurrent query serving | Production RAG systems ingest continuously; batch-only benchmarks are misleading |
| **Index Build Time** | Minutes/hours for full build or rebuild | Affects initial deployment, disaster recovery, and schema migration |
| **Cost** | TCO = Infrastructure + Ops Labor ($150/hr) + Over-provisioning buffer | The actual decision variable |
| **Operational Complexity** | Qualitative (Low/Medium/High) | Determines ongoing staffing and on-call burden |
| **Durability / HA** | Replication, multi-region, backup/restore capabilities | Non-negotiable for production at this investment level |

### A.2 Workload Profiles

| Profile | Vectors | Dimensionality | Sustained QPS | Filter Frequency | Example Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Small** | 1M | 1536 (OpenAI) | 10-50 | 50% filtered | Startup knowledge base |
| **Medium** | 100M | 768-1536 | 200-500 | 70% filtered | Multi-tenant SaaS RAG |
| **Large** | 1B | 768-1536 | 1,000-5,000 | 80% filtered | Enterprise search platform |

### A.3 Measurement Methodology Requirements

For any benchmark to be credible for this decision, it must satisfy:

1. **Equivalent hardware** across all systems (same EC2 instance type, same memory, same storage class)
2. **Real embeddings** (not random vectors) — Wikipedia/Cohere/OpenAI corpora preferred, as clustered real data behaves differently from uniform random data for IVF and HNSW
3. **Production settings enabled**: WAL on, replication factor ≥ 1, durability guarantees active
4. **Both filtered and unfiltered queries** measured separately
5. **Concurrent load** applied via multiple client threads (not single-threaded sequential)
6. **Warm-up period** before measurement; cold-start latency reported separately
7. **Recall measured at the same QPS target** (not maximum QPS at arbitrary recall)

### A.4 Provenance System

Every data point in Part B carries one of:

- 🟢 **Independent benchmark** — third-party, reproducible, linked
- 🟡 **Vendor-published** — linked, potential bias flagged explicitly
- 🟠 **Anecdotal / extrapolated** — methodology and assumptions stated
- 🔴 **DATA NOT AVAILABLE** — no credible source exists

---

## PART B: EMPIRICAL PERFORMANCE DATA

### B.1 Small Scale — 1M Vectors, 1536-dim, HNSW

#### B.1.1 Unfiltered Query Performance

| Database | P50 (ms) | P95 (ms) | P99 (ms) | QPS | Recall@10 | Source & Provenance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Qdrant** | ~2 | ~5 | ~8 | ~1,500 | 0.98 | 🟡 Qdrant benchmarks (2024), single-node, 1536-dim OpenAI embeddings |
| **Milvus** | ~4 | ~10 | ~15 | ~800–2,100 | 0.97–0.98 | 🟡 Range: low end from Qdrant bench, high end from VectorDBBench (10M, 768-dim, 8vCPU/32GB). Cross-vendor disagreement. |
| **Weaviate** | ~6 | ~15 | ~22 | ~600–1,100 | 0.96–0.97 | 🟡 Range: Qdrant bench (~600) vs VectorDBBench (~1,100). Go runtime overhead noted. |
| **pgvector 0.7 (HNSW)** | ~7 | ~25 | ~32 | ~380 | 0.97 | 🟡 Supabase benchmarks (8 vCPU, 32GB, 1M vectors, 1536-dim) |
| **Pinecone (pods, p2)** | ~8–15 | ~25–40 | ~50–80 | ~200/pod | ~0.95–0.98 | 🟠 Anecdotal user reports from blog posts (2023-2024). Pinecone publishes no reproducible benchmarks. |
| **Pinecone (Serverless)** | <50 (SLA) | — | <200 (inferred) | Not disclosed | Not disclosed | 🟡 Pinecone documentation/SLA only. Cold-start latency reported 200-800ms after idle (🟠 anecdotal). |

**Cross-validation note**: Qdrant appears in the top position in *both* its own vendor benchmark *and* the Zilliz/Milvus-affiliated VectorDBBench. This cross-validation from competing vendors lends moderate confidence to Qdrant's strong single-node performance. Milvus and Weaviate teams have disputed specific Qdrant benchmark configurations but have not published counter-benchmarks on identical hardware.

**ANN-Benchmarks context** 🟢: On glove-100 (1.2M vectors, 100-dim — lower dimensionality), hnswlib (Qdrant's underlying library) achieves ~1,200-1,500 QPS at 0.99 recall; pgvector HNSW achieves ~400-600 QPS; Weaviate ~800-1,100. These ratios are broadly consistent with the vendor benchmarks above, though the absolute numbers differ due to dimensionality (100 vs 1536) and dataset differences.

#### B.1.2 Filtered Search Performance (1% selectivity, 1M vectors)

| Database | QPS (filtered) | Recall@10 (filtered) | Filter Mechanism | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Qdrant** | ~1,200 | ~0.97 | Pre-filtering during HNSW traversal (payload index) | 🟡 Qdrant benchmarks |
| **Milvus** | ~350 | ~0.94 | Historically post-filtering; iterator-based improvements in v2.3+ | 🟡 Qdrant benchmarks (pre-Milvus 2.3 improvements) |
| **Weaviate** | ~180 | ~0.92 | Pre-filtering added in v1.18+; benchmark predates this | 🟡 Qdrant benchmarks (pre-v1.18 improvement) |
| **pgvector** | ~200–300 | ~0.95 | SQL WHERE clause + ANN (PostgreSQL planner handles filtering) | 🟠 Estimated; no formal filtered benchmark published |
| **Pinecone** | 🔴 DATA NOT AVAILABLE | 🔴 | Metadata filtering supported; ~20-40% QPS degradation reported | 🟠 Anecdotal |

**Critical RAG insight**: Filtered search is arguably more important than unfiltered for production RAG. Qdrant's architectural pre-filtering advantage is significant. However, both Weaviate (v1.18+) and Milvus (v2.3+) have shipped improvements that are **not yet reflected in any published benchmark**. The Qdrant-published filtered numbers for competitors may be stale.

#### B.1.3 pgvector Performance Degradation Curve

pgvector shows notable performance degradation with scale on single-node hardware 🟡 (Supabase benchmarks, 1536-dim, HNSW):

| Scale | QPS | P50 (ms) | P99 (ms) | Recall@10 | Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 250K | ~520 | ~4 | ~18 | 0.98 | 4 vCPU, 16GB |
| 1M | ~380 | ~7 | ~32 | 0.97 | 8 vCPU, 32GB |
| 5M | ~180 | ~15 | ~75 | 0.95 | 16 vCPU, 64GB |
| 10M | ~80 | ~35 | ~180 | 0.92 | 16 vCPU, 64GB |

This degradation curve is important: pgvector remains competitive at ≤1M but becomes increasingly uncompetitive beyond 5M vectors. Index build time at 10M: ~45 minutes.

---

### B.2 Medium Scale — 100M Vectors, 1536-dim

**🔴 CRITICAL DATA GAP: No independent benchmark exists for any system at 100M vectors.**

All entries below are architectural assessments and extrapolations, not empirical measurements.

| Database | Estimated QPS | Estimated P95 (ms) | Estimated Recall@10 | Architectural Readiness | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Milvus** (clustered) | ~500–1,000 | ~20–50 | ~0.95 | **Strongest**: Purpose-built disaggregated architecture (separate query/index/data nodes, etcd coordination, S3/MinIO storage layer). Most mature distributed design. | 🟠 Extrapolated from VectorDBBench 10M + Zilliz marketing materials |
| **Qdrant** (clustered, 3+ nodes) | 🔴 | 🔴 | 🔴 | **Good**: Raft-based consensus, sharding + replication since v1.1. Production-ready but fewer public references at this scale. | No data |
| **Weaviate** (clustered) | 🔴 | 🔴 | 🔴 | **Developing**: Raft-based replication (v1.25+), hash-based sharding. Relatively newer distributed implementation. | No data |
| **Pinecone Serverless** | 🔴 (specific QPS) | <50ms P50 (SLA claim) | 🔴 | **Claimed**: Proprietary disaggregated compute/storage. Claims 100M+ support. No architectural transparency for validation. | 🟡 Pinecone SLA only |
| **pgvector** | Not viable | Not viable | Not viable | Single-node PostgreSQL cannot handle ~600GB+ raw vectors (100M × 1536 × 4 bytes). Would require Citus/manual sharding with no published benchmarks or community precedent. | Architectural assessment |

**Architectural comparison at 100M**:

| Feature | Milvus | Qdrant | Weaviate | Pinecone |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Storage/Compute Disaggregation | ✅ (S3 + query nodes) | ❌ (coupled) | ❌ (coupled) | ✅ (claimed) |
| Independent Scaling of Read/Write | ✅ | Partial (add read replicas) | Partial | ✅ (claimed) |
| Segment-level Index Management | ✅ | ❌ (collection-level) | ❌ | Unknown |
| Multi-tenancy at Scale | ✅ (partition keys) | ✅ (collection-per-tenant or payload filtering) | ✅ (native multi-tenancy) | ✅ (namespaces) |

---

### B.3 Large Scale — 1B Vectors, 1536-dim

**🔴 No independent benchmark data exists for any system at 1B vectors.**

| Database | Data Available | Architectural Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| **Milvus / Zilliz Cloud** | 🔴 No published benchmark. Zilliz claims production deployments at 1B+. | Architecture explicitly designed for this scale: disaggregated storage (S3), streaming/batch separation, GPU index building support. Most credible candidate architecturally. |
| **Pinecone** | 🔴 No published benchmark. Claims "billions of vectors" support. | Proprietary serverless architecture. No transparency, but significant venture funding and enterprise customer base suggests real-world validation. |
| **Qdrant** | 🔴 No data. | Raft consensus may face coordination overhead at extreme shard counts. Unclear if validated at this scale. |
| **Weaviate** | 🔴 No data. | Newer distributed implementation. Unlikely to be the first choice at 1B without more maturity evidence. |
| **pgvector** | 🔴 Not architecturally viable. | Would require heroic custom engineering with no community support or precedent. |

---

### B.4 Index Build Time

| Database | 1M (1536-dim) | 10M | 100M | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **pgvector (HNSW)** | ~8–12 min | ~45 min | 🔴 Est. 8–15 hrs (single-node) | 🟡 Supabase |
| **Qdrant** | ~2–4 min | ~20–30 min | 🔴 | 🟠 User reports |
| **Milvus** | ~3–5 min | ~25–40 min | ~4–8 hrs (distributed) | 🟠 Zilliz documentation |
| **Weaviate** | ~3–5 min | ~30–45 min | 🔴 | 🟠 User reports |
| **Pinecone** | Abstracted | Abstracted | Abstracted | N/A (managed) |

---

### B

## Key Structural Issues Identified

### 1. Data Availability Gap (CRITICAL)
The task demands "ACTUAL benchmark numbers" across 5 databases × 3 scales × multiple metrics. Here is the honest landscape:

- **No single apples-to-apples benchmark exists** that covers all 5 systems at all 3 scales with identical hardware, dataset, and query distribution. This is the central problem of this report.
